evidence_items.evidence_description: EGFR L858R mutation predicts sensitivity to gefitinib treatment in NSCLC patients. This somatic mutation (CTG to CGG substitution at leucine-858 to arginine) was identified in tumors from patients who responded to gefitinib therapy and in lung adenocarcinoma cell lines that were hypersensitive to growth inhibition by gefitinib.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: EGFR mutations were found in additional lung cancer samples from U.S. patients who responded to gefitinib therapy and in a lung adenocarcinoma cell line that was hypersensitive to growth inhibition by gefitinib
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is a landmark paper establishing EGFR mutations as predictive biomarkers for gefitinib response. The L858R mutation is specifically identified and linked to clinical response, making this highly actionable for treatment decisions.

evidence_items.evidence_description: EGFR exon 19 deletions (including 15-nucleotide and 24-nucleotide deletions at codons 746-750 and 752-759) predict sensitivity to gefitinib in NSCLC patients. Multiple deletion variants (Del-1 through Del-5) were identified in patients who showed tumor regression with gefitinib treatment.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Treatment with the EGFR kinase inhibitor gefitinib (Iressa) causes tumor regression in some patients with NSCLC, more frequently in Japan. EGFR mutations were found in additional lung cancer samples from U.S. patients who responded to gefitinib therapy
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Multiple exon 19 deletion variants (Del-1 through Del-5) are identified in the paper and associated with gefitinib response. These deletions are critical predictive biomarkers for EGFR TKI therapy.

evidence_items.evidence_description: EGFR G719S mutation (glycine-719 to serine) is associated with gefitinib sensitivity in lung adenocarcinoma. This somatic mutation was identified among EGFR variants found in gefitinib-responsive tumors but not in gefitinib-insensitive tumors or cell lines.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: EGFR mutations were found in additional lung cancer samples from U.S. patients who responded to gefitinib therapy and in a lung adenocarcinoma cell line that was hypersensitive to growth inhibition by gefitinib, but not in gefitinib-insensitive tumors or cell lines
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: G719S is specifically listed as one of the EGFR mutations identified in this study. The paper establishes that EGFR mutations predict gefitinib sensitivity, making this actionable for treatment selection.

evidence_items.evidence_description: Absence of EGFR mutations (wildtype EGFR) is associated with lack of response to gefitinib in NSCLC. EGFR mutations were not found in gefitinib-insensitive tumors or cell lines, indicating that wildtype EGFR predicts resistance or lack of response to gefitinib therapy.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: EGFR mutations were found in additional lung cancer samples from U.S. patients who responded to gefitinib therapy and in a lung adenocarcinoma cell line that was hypersensitive to growth inhibition by gefitinib, but not in gefitinib-insensitive tumors or cell lines
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: The paper explicitly states that EGFR mutations were NOT found in gefitinib-insensitive samples, providing negative predictive evidence that wildtype EGFR is associated with resistance.

plan.key_variants: EGFR L858R, EGFR G719S, EGFR exon 19 deletions (Del-1: codons 746-750, Del-2: codons 752-759, Del-3, Del-4, Del-5), wild-type EGFR
plan.key_diseases: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Lung adenocarcinoma, Bronchioloalveolar carcinoma
plan.focus_sections: Abstract (core findings), Results (mutation frequencies, clinical correlations, patient response data), Methods (patient selection and response criteria), Tables (mutation-response correlations), Discussion (clinical implications)
plan.extraction_notes: This is a landmark 2004 paper discovering EGFR mutations as predictive biomarkers for gefitinib response in NSCLC. Priority evidence items: (1) EGFR L858R predicts gefitinib sensitivity, (2) EGFR exon 19 deletions predict gefitinib sensitivity, (3) Wild-type EGFR associated with gefitinib resistance. Look for specific patient response data, mutation frequencies by geography (Japan vs US), cell line validation data, and objective response rates. The paper should contain tables correlating specific mutations with clinical responses. Extract both Predictive (gefitinib response) and potentially Diagnostic (mutation frequency) evidence. Note geographic/ethnic differences in mutation prevalence. Each deletion variant (Del-1 through Del-5) may warrant separate evidence items if clinical data supports them individually.

final_critique.item_feedback:
All 4 evidence items are APPROVED:

**Item 1 - EGFR L858R → Gefitinib Sensitivity (NSCLC):**
✅ Excellent quality. L858R specifically identified in variants list, verbatim quote accurate, all required fields present, appropriate evidence type/significance/direction. Clinically actionable for treatment selection.

**Item 2 - EGFR EXON 19 DELETION → Gefitinib Sensitivity (NSCLC):**
✅ Strong evidence item. Comprehensive coverage of multiple deletion variants (Del-1 through Del-5) mentioned in paper. Verbatim quotes verified. Avoids redundancy by consolidating all exon 19 deletions into one item. All technical requirements met.

**Item 3 - EGFR G719S → Gefitinib Sensitivity (Lung Adenocarcinoma):**
✅ Well-supported. G719S specifically listed in variants, appropriate disease specification (lung adenocarcinoma mentioned in verbatim quote), all fields properly populated. Actionable for clinical decision-making.

**Item 4 - EGFR WILDTYPE → Gefitinib Resistance (NSCLC):**
✅ Important negative evidence. Correctly uses DOES_NOT_SUPPORT direction with RESISTANCE significance. Paper explicitly states mutations were "not in gefitinib-insensitive tumors or cell lines." All technical requirements satisfied. Provides valuable clinical information about lack of response.
final_critique.missing_items:
No critical evidence is missing. The 4 items comprehensively capture the key findings from this landmark paper:
- Two major activating mutation types (L858R and exon 19 deletions) predicting sensitivity
- One additional uncommon mutation (G719S) predicting sensitivity
- Wildtype status predicting resistance/lack of response

These represent the core clinical contributions of the Paez et al. 2004 Science paper establishing EGFR mutations as predictive biomarkers for gefitinib therapy in NSCLC.
final_critique.extra_items:
No extra or problematic items remain. The Extractor successfully removed all 6 items flagged in the previous critique:
- Removed Item 4 (prevalence data - not actionable)
- Removed Item 5 (DEL 746-750 - redundant with exon 19 deletion)
- Removed Item 6 (DEL 752-759 - redundant with exon 19 deletion)
- Removed Item 8 (L858R in adenocarcinoma - redundant with NSCLC item)
- Removed Item 9 (BAC evidence - insufficient specific data)
- Removed Item 10 (diagnostic prevalence - inappropriate evidence type)

The final extraction is focused, non-redundant, and high-quality.
final_critique.summary:
APPROVE - Excellent revision. All 4 evidence items meet CIViC quality standards.

The Extractor has successfully addressed all feedback from the previous critique, reducing from 10 items to 4 high-quality, non-redundant evidence items. Each item:
- Contains all 8 required fields
- Has accurate verbatim quotes verified in the paper content
- Uses appropriate evidence types, directions, and significance values
- Is clinically actionable for treatment decisions
- Is properly supported by the source material

The extraction captures the landmark findings of this 2004 Science paper that established EGFR mutations as predictive biomarkers for gefitinib response in lung cancer. The items cover the major mutation types (L858R, exon 19 deletions, G719S) and importantly include negative evidence (wildtype = resistance).

No further revision needed. This extraction is ready for CIViC submission.

paper_content.title: EGFR Mutations in Lung Cancer: Correlation with Clinical Response to Gefitinib Therapy
paper_content.authors: J. Guillermo Paez, Pasi A. Jänne, Jeffrey C. Lee, Sean Tracy, Heidi Greulich, Stacey Gabriel, Paula Herman, Frederic J. Kaye, Neal Lindeman, Titus J. Boggon, Katsuhiko Naoki, Hidefumi Sasaki, Yoshitaka Fujii, Michael J. Eck, William R. Sellers, Bruce E. Johnson, Matthew Meyerson
paper_content.abstract: Receptor tyrosine kinase genes were sequenced in non–small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and matched normal tissue. Somatic mutations of the epidermal growth factor receptor gene EGFR were found in 15 of 58 unselected tumors from Japan and 1 of 61 from the United States. Treatment with the EGFR kinase inhibitor gefitinib (Iressa) causes tumor regression in some patients with NSCLC, more frequently in Japan. EGFR mutations were found in additional lung cancer samples from U.S. patients who responded to gefitinib therapy and in a lung adenocarcinoma cell line that was hypersensitive to growth inhibition by gefitinib, but not in gefitinib-insensitive tumors or cell lines. These results suggest that EGFR mutations may predict sensitivity to gefitinib.
paper_content.genes: EGFR (Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor), BRAF, PIK3CA, c-kit, ERK1/2 (extracellular signal-regulated kinase), AKT (v-akt murine thymoma viral oncogene homolog)
paper_content.variants: EGFR L858R (CTG→CGG, leucine-858 to arginine), EGFR G719S (glycine-719 to serine), EGFR Del-1 (15-nucleotide deletion, codons 746-750, starting nucleotide 2235 or 2236), EGFR Del-2 (24-nucleotide deletion, codons 752-759), EGFR Del-3 (in-frame deletion with amino acid substitution), EGFR Del-4 (in-frame deletion with amino acid substitution), EGFR Del-5 (homozygous in-frame deletion), BRAF G463, BRAF L596
paper_content.diseases: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Lung adenocarcinoma, Bronchioloalveolar carcinoma, Melanoma, Colorectal carcinoma, Ovarian carcinoma, Glioblastoma, Chronic myelogenous leukemia, Gastrointestinal stromal tumors
paper_content.therapies: Gefitinib (Iressa) - EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor, Imatinib - tyrosine kinase inhibitor (referenced for c-kit)
paper_content.clinical_trials: Multi-institutional phase II trial of gefitinib comparing Japanese (27.5% response) vs European-derived population (10.4% response); Dana-Farber Cancer Institute expanded access program and post-regulatory approval treatment (>125 patients, 5 responders and 4 progressors analyzed)
